entry.title: AI‑Driven Procurement Analytics with SAP S/4HANA, SAP Analytics Cloud, and Joule
entry.content:
AI‑Driven Procurement Analytics with SAP S/4HANA, SAP Analytics Cloud, and Joule

Procurement teams are under pressure to go beyond static reports and deliver proactive, data‑driven recommendations. SAP now offers an end‑to‑end Business AI stack that can do exactly that  from embedded analytics in SAP S/4HANA to AI‑assisted insights in SAP Analytics Cloud and generative AI with Joule.

This post explains how SAP Business AI supports procurement analytics across the SAP portfolio and how customers can combine native capabilities with external AI where required  fully aligned with SAP’s official strategy and product documentation.

SAP Business AI in a Procurement Context

SAP Business AI is SAP’s integrated suite of AI capabilities delivered across applications and SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). It is designed to work on reliable business data, follow industry and regulatory standards, and be embedded directly into end‑to‑end processes such as source‑to‑pay.

According to SAP’s Business AI positioning:

  • SAP offers “AI and agents that actually understand all your business processes and data,” with a “single AI interface that seamlessly integrates data and workflows across your SAP and non‑SAP applications.” Also refer to SAP Business AI Roadmaps 
  • Customers can “drive efficiency across every business function with access to over 230 AI-powered scenarios expanding to 400 by the end of 2025,” including finance, supply chain, and procurement use cases.
  • SAP BTP provides AI foundation services and tools to build, run, and integrate advanced AI solutions and agents at scale.

For procurement, these capabilities manifest in three main layers:

  1. Embedded analytics and AI inside SAP S/4HANA and SAP’s spend‑management solutions.
  2. AI‑assisted analytics and planning in SAP Analytics Cloud.
  3. Generative AI and copilots such as Joule, running across the portfolio.

Embedded Analytics and AI in SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA Cloud includes powerful embedded analytics with SAP Fiori analytical apps, CDS‑based data models, and built‑in AI features. SAP describes these as “AI‑driven interactions” that help users improve productivity and obtain quick insights directly in the operational system.

Key characteristics for procurement:

  • Real‑time visibility on live transactional data from purchasing documents, suppliers, and materials.
  • SAP Fiori analytical apps and KPI tiles for purchasing performance, open items, supplier reliability, and more, which are part of the standard S/4HANA content.
  • Integration into Fiori elements and analytical floor plans that support drill‑down, filtering, and smart visualizations.

These capabilities are ideal for operational and tactical decisions (for example, monitoring overdue POs or supplier delivery performance) and provide a governed, SAP‑delivered starting point for procurement analytics. They can be combined with additional AI services via SAP BTP if customers need more advanced data science workloads.

Official reference: “Enabling new, AI-driven interactions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud with SAP Fiori elements” (SAP asset).

SAP Analytics Cloud: AI‑Assisted Analytics and Planning

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) is SAP’s strategic solution for analytics and planning and is a central pillar of SAP Business AI for analytics scenarios.

The SAC product page describes how customers can:

  • “Embrace the power of generative AI to automate reporting, discover hidden insights, and develop business plans with Joule copilot.”
  • “Use AI to automatically reveal relationships in data, hidden patterns, and outliers, and to predict potential outcomes and forecasts.”
  • Bring together data from SAP S/4HANA, SAP HANA, SAP Datasphere, and other sources for BI, planning, and predictive analysis in a single experience.

The “Artificial Intelligence for Planning & Analytics” and AI‑assisted features guides describe specific SAC capabilities for business users:

  • Smart Insights, Smart Discovery, Smart Predict  ML‑driven features that help users understand key influencers, detect patterns, and generate predictive forecasts.
  • Search to Insight / Just Ask  natural language query interfaces that allow users to ask questions in plain language and receive visualizations or answers.
  • AI‑assisted features  such as AI‑generated calculations, chart summaries, and explanations integrated into core workflows.

These capabilities are directly integrated into the user interface and can be enabled and governed centrally by administrators. For procurement, this means that category managers or buyers can:

  • Explore spend patterns and outliers using Smart Discovery.
  • Ask natural language questions about spend, supplier performance, or forecasted volumes using Just Ask.
  • Build plans and scenarios with AI‑supported forecasting and explanation features.

Joule: SAP’s Generative AI Copilot

Joule is SAP’s generative AI copilot and is a key element of SAP Business AI. SAP describes Joule as a “single AI interface” that operates across SAP solutions, helping users get more done faster by understanding both business data and processes. For detailed information about Joule capabilities and setup, visit the Joule Help Portal and explore the Joule Community Hub for implementation guidance and best practices.

In analytics and planning, the SAC product page states that customers can use Joule to “automate reporting, discover hidden insights, and develop business plans” by interacting with analytics content using natural language. On the Business AI community pages, SAP highlights that Joule can complete navigational and transactional tasks significantly faster for users across different lines of business.

From a procurement‑analytics perspective, Joule enables scenarios such as:

  • Conversational questions on procurement KPIs (for example, spend by category, supplier performance trends, or contract utilization) with answers grounded in SAC models and S/4HANA data.
  • Narrative summaries of charts, dashboards, and plans, helping stakeholders quickly understand the drivers behind spend or forecast changes.
  • Assistance in creating or adapting analytic content and planning models more efficiently.
  • For a complete list of procurement use cases and implementation details, see the Joule Capabilities Guide for Sourcing and Procurement.

SAP Business AI for Procurement: Spend and Supplier Intelligence

SAP describes “AI in procurement” and “AI for procurement” as a set of Business AI capabilities embedded across SAP’s spend‑management portfolio. According to SAP, these solutions help procurement organizations to:

  • Analyse and optimize spend  AI‑driven spend analysis to identify spending patterns, uncover savings opportunities, and support category strategy decisions.
  • Enhance sourcing and supplier management  evaluate supplier performance, risk, and sustainability and recommend suitable suppliers based on a combination of internal and external data.
  • Improve compliance and risk control  automatically check purchases against contracts and policies, and detect anomalies or suspicious patterns.
  • Automate document processing  use document AI to extract, classify, and validate information from invoices, orders, and contracts, reducing manual effort and errors.

Examples from SAP’s official resources include:

  • “AI in procurement: A complete guide,” which outlines how AI supports spend analysis, sourcing, contract compliance, and supplier risk management.
  • “Discover Advanced AI Procurement Solutions | SAP Business AI,” which summarizes AI‑based spend analytics, supplier insights, and process automation capabilities within SAP’s spend‑management offerings.
  • SAP Document AI, an end‑to‑end intelligent document processing solution that can be used to automate document‑heavy procurement processes (for example, invoice processing).

These domain‑specific AI capabilities provide out‑of‑the‑box value for procurement organizations and are designed to work together with embedded analytics and SAC.

Extending with SAP BTP and AI Foundation

For customers with requirements that go beyond standard content and embedded AI, SAP BTP provides the foundation to build and operate custom AI scenarios.

Key elements include:

  • SAP AI Foundation and AI services  a portfolio of services on SAP BTP (for example, SAP AI Core) to develop, deploy, and manage AI applications.
  • AI units and Business AI Catalog  a consumption‑based model to access SAP Business AI services consistently across SAP solutions, along with a catalog that lists available AI capabilities and their technical and commercial details.
  • Integration with SAP and non‑SAP data  secure connectivity and APIs to combine SAP data with external data sources and expose results back to S/4HANA, SAC, or other applications.

The SAP Business AI onboarding resources describe how organizations can govern AI usage and track AI unit consumption across services. This provides a controlled way to integrate external AI technologies while keeping SAP BTP as the core platform for AI operations and lifecycle management.

Putting It Together: A Reference Architecture for Procurement Analytics

Based on SAP’s official positioning, a typical architecture for AI‑driven procurement analytics with SAP Business AI can look like this:

  1. Operational analytics in SAP S/4HANA
    • Use embedded analytics and AI‑driven Fiori apps on purchasing documents, suppliers, and materials to monitor day‑to‑day performance on live data.
  2. Advanced analytics and planning in SAP Analytics Cloud
    • Connect to S/4HANA, SAP Datasphere, and other sources to perform AI‑assisted analysis (Smart Insights, Smart Discovery, Just Ask) and planning on procurement data.
  3. Generative AI with Joule
    • Enable Joule as a copilot across SAC and other SAP cloud solutions so buyers and category managers can ask natural‑language questions, generate narratives, and automate routine analytics tasks.
  4. Procurement‑specific Business AI
    • Activate AI capabilities within SAP’s spend‑management solutions (for example, AI‑driven spend analysis, supplier risk, document AI) to address domain‑specific scenarios.
  5. Extensions on SAP BTP
    • Use SAP BTP and AI Foundation to develop custom AI models, agents, or integrations where customers want to combine SAP data with external AI services or build very specialized use cases, managed via AI units and the Business AI Catalog.

This architecture keeps SAP as the system of record and governance layer while allowing organizations to add external AI techniques where helpful, always mediated through SAP BTP and SAP Business AI.

How to Get Started

Customers who want to advance their procurement analytics with SAP Business AI can take the following steps, fully aligned with SAP’s guidance:

  • Review the SAP Business AI white paper and product page to understand SAP’s overall AI strategy and how it applies to their landscape.
  • Explore the Business AI Catalog and Business AI use‑case library to identify relevant procurement scenarios and estimate AI unit consumption.
  • Activate and adopt embedded analytics in SAP S/4HANA and AI‑assisted features in SAP Analytics Cloud, including Just Ask and Smart features, for procurement‑related models.
  • Work with their SAP account team and Customer Engagement Executive to clarify entitlements, AI unit model, and roadmap for Joule and procurement‑specific AI services in their region and product portfolio.

With this approach, procurement organizations can move step‑by‑step from static reporting to AI‑assisted insights and, ultimately, to generative AI copilots embedded in their daily workflows  all within the boundaries of SAP’s officially supported platforms and services.

Example : ME2M‑Style Open PO Analysis

Many procurement teams, including yours, routinely analyse outputs from classic reports such as ME2M (open purchase orders) to understand purchasing behaviour, policy exceptions, open commitments, and other operational patterns. SAP’s own guidance on procurement analytics and Business AI maps well to this type of use case.

At a high level, the ME2M‑style analysis could be re‑implemented using SAP’s native layers as follows:

Step / question

SAP capability to use

What it does for ME2M‑type data

“Can we analyse open PO data without exporting to external tools?”

S/4HANA Procurement Analytics and Embedded Analytics (CDS‑based virtual data model and Fiori analytical apps)

Provides real‑time views on purchasing documents and open items using analytical floor plans and overview pages, without data extracts.

“Can we see patterns, anomalies, and drivers in this data?”

SAP Analytics Cloud Smart Discovery, Smart Insights, and predictive features on a ME2M‑equivalent data model

Automatically surfaces key drivers, outliers, and trends in open PO and commitment data modelled from S/4HANA into SAC.

“Can users ask questions in natural language instead of building queries?”

SAP Analytics Cloud “Just Ask” / Search to Insight and Joule copilot

Allows buyers and analysts to ask questions about open POs (for example, by supplier, material, or plant) and get governed, visual answers.

“Can we connect this to more advanced or custom AI if needed?”

SAP BTP AI services and SAP Business AI for Procurement

Enables domain‑specific AI (spend/supplier insights) and side‑by‑side custom models that work on curated procurement data sets.

Concretely, S/4HANA Cloud procurement analytics already provides embedded analytical content (such as the Procurement Overview page, open purchase order‑related tiles, and supplier evaluation) that runs on live procurement data without extraction. This gives you a governed starting point for open PO visibility that covers much of what users currently pull from ME2M.

You can then expose the relevant CDS‑based procurement data into SAP Datasphere or directly into SAP Analytics Cloud and apply AI‑assisted features there. Using Smart Discovery and Smart Insights, teams can look for patterns such as recurring min–max policy breaches, unusual lead times, or atypical price or quantity behaviour in the open PO population.

Finally, with Joule and “Just Ask” connected to SAP Analytics Cloud, buyers can ask natural‑language questions such as “Show me open PO value by supplier with overdue items” or “Which materials have the highest number of min–max violations this month?” and receive answers grounded in the SAC models built on S/4HANA data. For more advanced needs, SAP Business AI for Procurement and SAP BTP‑based AI services provide additional options to detect risk patterns and optimize spend, still using SAP as the system of record.

Information Accuracy

  • Information presented reflects publicly available SAP documentation as of April 2026 and may not reflect the most current product capabilities or availability.
  • Readers should verify current product features, licensing requirements, and regional availability through official SAP channels before making implementation decisions.

entry.title: Cloud Integration with Translation Hub
entry.content:
Introduction:

In this blog post, I will demonstrate how to connect Cloud Integration with Translation Hub. As a use case, consider a scenario where a sender system submits an order request. If the order creation fails, the system expects the error message to be returned in both English and Japanese.

 

Translation Hub:

For an overview of the SAP Translation Hub service and its features, refer to discovery center.

 

Step 1:

Create an instance for a service named SAP Translation Hub (technical name: document-translation) and generate its corresponding service key.

From the service key, retrieve ‘documenttranslation.url’, ‘uaa.clientid’, ‘uaa.clientsecret’ and ‘uaa.url’, which will be later used in integration scenario.

Step 2:

Explore document translation api at Business Accelerator Hub.

For the model parameter, two values are supported: default and llm. The default model uses SAP-trained Neural Machine Translation (NMT). For a list of supported languages, refer to the SAP Translation Hub documentation: Supported Languages

The llm falls under the generative AI category and can be used when a target language is not supported by the SAP-trained NMT model. For example, to translate text into Bengali.

 

Cloud Integration:

Step 1:

Create a security material of type ‘OAuth2 Client Credentials’.

Step 2:

Design the iFlow as illustrated below.

The Content Modifier is used to set the message header and body.  As only the translated text is required, the ‘Accept’ header is set to ‘application/octet-stream’.

The request body is configured as shown below:

Configure the HTTP receiver adapter to connect with document translation api as shown below.

Step 3:

Test the iFlow endpoint using an API testing tool. In the request payload, ‘orderId’ is a mandatory field as defined in the XML Validator step; therefore, any payload that does not include ‘orderId’ is routed to an exception subprocess.

Please note that all the data are fictitious.

 

Conclusion:

This blog post demonstrates a prototype for integrating Translation Hub with Cloud Integration.

entry.title: Harnessing SAP Business Data Cloud for the administrator persona
entry.content:
Working with SAP Partners on SAP Business Data Cloud for the administrator role brings a very concrete set of tasks that often get overlooked until go-live.

In this session, we walk through how administration looks in real projects, from provisioning to connectivity with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition.

We will cover what's new in the landscape, how SAP for Me supports provisioning, and how integration between S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP Business Data Cloud is set up.  Plus a live Q&A to address practical questions.

The thing is, many discussions stay at concept level.  Here we focus on what you do: moving from customer landscape view to instantiation, setting trust, and ensuring bi-directional connectivity works as expected.

Finally, you will be able to guide customer conversations on administration topics and explain the current landscape changes with confidence.

entry.title: How SAP QA Testing Services Reduce Downtime in Large Enterprises
entry.content:
Understanding the True Cost and Causes of SAP Downtime

Downtime in SAP environments is rarely caused by a single defect. It is often the result of multiple gaps across validation layers, integration points, and release practices. Without structured SAP testing services, these gaps accumulate and surface during peak business operations.

One of the most significant contributing factors to failures is a lack of complete process validation, which generally only utilizes limited functional testing to validate enterprise workflows with standard case scenarios. Since enterprise workflows include exceptions, dependencies, and real-time data exchange with other systems/locations, if these workflows are not completely validated through comprehensive testing, they are likely to produce errors when used.

Inadequate regression coverage is another major contributor to SAP downtime. SAP systems frequently receive updates, patches, and configuration changes, and if these updates and changes are not properly tested through thorough SAP regression testing services, they may render otherwise stable processes unstable. Examples of SAP regression testing services not properly executed include manually executing regression tests, therefore. potentially missing business-critical regression tests leading to a greater risk of downtime after executing major framework releases.

Integration complexity further amplifies the problem. Modern SAP solutions communicate with external 3rd party solutions via multiple APIs or applications. When there are no SAP integration testing services in place, there is a higher probability of data inconsistencies and workflow disruptions. Also, problems caused by integration between modern SAP and older SAP applications are often difficult to discover in isolated testing environments, while causing a significant negative impact on production systems.

The lack of automation further compounds the problem. Due to the absence of SAP test automation services, SAP testing cycles are slow and inconsistent. Time constraints often force teams to skip critical validations, thus allowing defects to escape into production. Together, all the gaps explain why downtime persists despite significant investments in SAP infrastructure.

Core Components of SAP QA Testing That Prevent Downtime

Reducing downtime requires a layered testing approach where each component addresses a specific risk area. SAP testing services create a measurable impact by combining multiple validation techniques into a unified and structured framework, ensuring systems remain stable under real business conditions.

Functional Validation for Business Accuracy

SAP functional testing ensures that core business processes operate exactly as intended in real scenarios. It validates critical workflows such as order processing, billing, and inventory updates, while also covering exception handling and edge cases that are often overlooked. This structured validation reduces the risk of process failures during live operations and keeps business continuity intact.

Regression Stability Across Releases

Frequent system updates require continuous and reliable validation. SAP regression testing services ensure that existing functionalities remain stable and unaffected after every change or enhancement. With automated regression suites improving both coverage and execution speed, organizations can release updates with confidence while minimizing disruptions to ongoing operations.

Integration Assurance Across Systems

Enterprise systems depend heavily on seamless integration. SAP integration testing services validate end-to-end data flow between SAP and connected external systems, identifying inconsistencies in data formats, APIs, and workflows. This prevents failures caused by system dependencies and ensures that all interconnected processes function without disruption.

Automation for Speed and Consistency

Manual testing alone cannot keep up with the scale and speed of modern SAP environments. SAP test automation services enable faster, repeatable, and consistent test execution across large and complex test suites. This not only supports continuous testing in fast-paced release cycles but also improves overall testing efficiency and reliability.

Role of Advanced Testing Strategies in Downtime Reduction

A structured testing approach requires more than tools and execution. It depends on well-defined strategies that align testing with business risk and system complexity. SAP testing services extend into planning and optimization through targeted methodologies that directly influence system stability.

One critical aspect is risk-based testing. Instead of testing everything equally, teams prioritize scenarios that directly impact business continuity. This approach aligns with SAP test strategy services, where high-risk transactions receive deeper validation. It ensures that testing efforts are focused and effective rather than exhaustive but inefficient.

Another important strategy is early validation within the development lifecycle. By integrating SAP functional testing at early stages, defects are identified before they propagate into later phases. This reduces rework and prevents production failures that often arise from late-stage detection. Early validation also improves coordination between development and testing teams.

Continuous testing plays an equally important role. With the support of SAP regression testing services and SAP test automation services, validation becomes an ongoing activity instead of a release phase task. Automated pipelines ensure that every change is tested in near real time, reducing the risk of introducing instability into production environments.

Measurable Business Benefits of SAP Testing Services

A well-structured SAP testing approach does more than identifying defects. It strengthens system stability and drives consistent operational performance.

Faster and Safer Releases

Frequent updates demand stability. Regression testing ensures existing processes remain unaffected, while automation reduces execution time. The result is quicker releases without compromising validation depth.

Improved System Reliability

Reliable systems depend on consistent validation. Functional testing confirms transaction accuracy, and integration testing ensures smooth data exchange. Together, they reduce inconsistencies that often surface post go-live.

Optimized Resource Utilization

Manual testing limits scalability. Automation shifts the focus toward complex scenarios instead of repetitive tasks. This not only improves efficiency but also allows teams to use their time more strategically.

Better Decision Making

Testing is not just execution; it is insight generation. SAP testing highlights risk areas and system gaps, giving teams the clarity needed to plan releases, prioritize fixes, and avoid costly errors.

Best Practices for Implementing SAP QA Testing at Scale

Scaling SAP testing requires more than tools. It demands a disciplined approach that aligns testing efforts with business priorities and system complexity.

Establish a Risk-Based Testing Framework

Not all processes carry equal weight. Prioritizing high-impact workflows ensures critical business functions are tested thoroughly, reducing the chances of failure where it matters most.

Build Strong Regression Coverage

Every system change introduces risk. A well-defined regression strategy ensures stability is maintained across releases, while automation helps sustain consistency without increasing effort.

Strengthen Integration Validation

Modern SAP environments rely on interconnected systems. Validating APIs, data flows, and dependencies ensures seamless communication and prevents breakdowns caused by integration gaps.

Scale Automation Across Testing Layers

Automation should not be limited to basic checks. Expanding it across functional and integration layers improves speed, increases coverage, and brings consistency to large-scale testing efforts.

Collaborate with the Right Testing Partner

Expertise makes a difference. A capable SAP testing partner brings structured frameworks, domain knowledge, and proven practices, ensuring long-term stability and reducing operational risks.

Final Say

Downtime in large SAP systems should be regarded as a business risk that impacts revenue, compliance, and operational continuity rather than just a technical issue. Therefore, it's critical to take a systematic approach to this problem using SAP testing services to create proactive control mechanisms rather than traditional support functions.

entry.title: Update on SAP HANA Cloud positioning and commercialization in context of SAP Business Data Cloud
entry.content:
SAP Partners, we are hosting a focused live session: Update on SAP HANA Cloud positioning and commercialization in context of SAP Business Data Cloud.

In this session, we walk through how SAP HANA Cloud fits inside SAP Business Data Cloud and what this means for your architecture decisions and client conversations.

We will cover three key areas.  First, how SAP HANA Cloud supports scenarios around agentic AI and custom AI-infused applications.  Second, how the commercial model is integrated within SAP Business Data Cloud.  Third, the main technical differences when accessing SAP HANA Cloud via SAP Business Technology Platform versus SAP Business Data Cloud.

We will also look ahead at upcoming changes in the roadmap so you can align your planning with what is coming next.

If you work in data, analytics, or application development in the SAP ecosystem, this session helps you sharpen how you position SAP HANA Cloud with your customers.

https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/apr/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013682.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPAnalyticsCloud #DataArchitecture #CloudData

entry.title: SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) Onboarding
entry.content:
In this blog we will give a brief outline as to the onboarding process for SAP BDC, point to the many online resources and provide details on how you can get 1:1 assistance from an SAP onboarding specialist. 

Target audience

  • You may be a direct employee of the SAP customer who is tasked with provisioning the system.
  • You may be a third party consultancy, engaged by the SAP customer, who is tasked with provisioning the system.

Prerequisites

  1. The BDC license is purchased, and you have received the welcome email. After your organization has purchased SAP Business Data Cloud, the user designated as the contact person receives an invitation email with a link to SAP for Me. If you’re unsure about your registration as a contact person for SAP Business Data Cloud, contact your Sales Account Executive or the Customer Interaction Center (CIC).

    If you are from a third party of the SAP customer, then reach out to your contact on the SAP customer side. They must provide you with this email. Note: Do not open a support Case with SAP Support as they cannot resend or provide you with this email.

  2. You will also require an S-user with Super Admin or Cloud Admin access in SAP for Me. Refer to KBA 2492132 What is the difference between Super, Cloud, and User Administrator S-user IDs? - SAP for Me and more information available here Understanding S-Users and SAP for Me

Outline Steps

  1. Provisioning the BDC Cockpit is the first step and you cannot proceed to provision anything else until this step is completed. We provide step by step instructions in the Community Blog Part 1: Provisioning of Business Data Cloud through SAP4Me
  2. Then provision the BDC Core components of Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), Databricks as per your business requirements. Refer to steps in Provisioning SAP Business Data Cloud Applications

Resources

Before embarking on the provisioning process, it is important to educate yourself on the entire process.

  1. The Business Data Cloud Resource Center is your starting point for this journey



    There are many resources available here including: 
    1. the SAP BDC Onboarding Webcast which should be mandatory viewing for anyone who is about to provision SAP BDC.
    2. full video of the provisioning process here: Provisioning of SAP Business Data Cloud and its core components
    3. SAP BDC Onboarding – this is the official product documentation which explains in detail the entire provisioning process.
    4. SAP BDC Community Blog series here Introductory Guide to Provisioning of SAP Business Data Cloud – this has a series of blogs with screenshots showing the step by step process.

  2. If you still have questions or if you would like to have an SAP onboarding expert walk you through the provisioning process, you can book a 1:1 onboarding session with SAP via SAP Business Data Cloud -> “If you still have questions, request a 1:1 onboarding session here”.

  3. If you encounter technical issues during the actual provisioning process, then you may open a Support Case via the SAP for Me portal.
  4. If you have questions outside of SAP Technical Support area of responsibility or if you would like to leverage the knowledge and experience of other SAP BDC Administrators and users, post your questions to the SAP Business Data Cloud - SAP Community Q&A space.

Additional Resources

  • Official Product documentation here: SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Community Blog SAP Business Data Cloud - From Provisioning to Intelligent Applications
  • SAP Community Blog SAP Business Data Cloud - FAQs
  • SAP Community Blog Rewiring of SAP Datasphere to SAP Business Data Cloud
  • KBA 3733691 SAP BDC Onboarding FAQ
  • KBA 2626344 Who is the SAP Account Manager/Executive for my company?
  • KBA 3705747 Provisioning failure due to duplicate host name during SAP Business Data Cloud Cockpit

entry.title: Unlocking custom AI use-cases with SAP HANA Cloud
entry.content:
If you’re working with data in SAP landscapes, you already know the pressure to turn it into real outcomes.  The good news.  You don’t need to move your data or add complexity to start building AI use cases.

In this upcoming session, we’ll walk through how to use the built-in AI capabilities of SAP HANA Cloud to create practical, scalable solutions.  From AutoML with PAL to new Data and Discovery Agents, you’ll see how to go from raw data to actionable insights in a structured way.

Plus, we’ll share an early look at Tabular AI with SAP-RPT-1.  This is where things start to get interesting for partners building next-gen applications.

The focus is simple.  Clear steps.  Real examples.  Immediate value.

Join us and leave with a plan you can apply right away.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013362.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #AI #DataAnalytics #MachineLearning

entry.title: Integration capabilities highly rated in SAP BTP reviews
entry.content:
Only a few short months ago I posted a blog in celebration of SAP Business Technology Platform's (SAP BTP) performance in G2’s Winter 2026 awards for their Cloud Platform as a Service (PaaS) category. Now having quickly accelerated to their Spring 2026 awards, once again I am happy to report that SAP BTP featured prominently, achieving leader badges along with a leaders position in the G2 Enterprise Grid for CPaaS.  

 Connections count!

Reading just a handful of the most recent SAP BTP reviews on the G2 website reveals just how important robust yet flexible integration is to SAP customers. Among the most recent customer reviews, many reveal Integration as something for which they rate SAP BTP highly:

  • 18 April 2026 I use SAP Business Technology Platform for data integration, analytics, and app development. It connects multiple systems, reduces data silos, and streamlines workflows, providing real-time insights. Its scalability handles large data efficiently. 
  • 16 April 2026 SAP Business Technology Platform helps address the challenge of integrating, extending, and modernising SAP landscapes by offering a unified platform for integration, analytics, data management, and application development. For us, this means simpler system integration, better visibility through analytics, and faster innovation without disrupting our core SAP systems. 
  • 10 April 2026 By utilizing the SAP Integration Suite, we successfully automated the data flow between our core ERP and third-party tools. The biggest benefit is the dramatic reduction in manual IT maintenance, saving us countless hours each month. 
  • 16 March 2026 SAP Business Technology Platform helps us connect systems, manage data, and build extensions for SAP apps without changing the core system, keeping it stable. It speeds up development by allowing integration and new functions, ensuring easy modifications without risking the main setup. 
  • 12 February 2026  SAP technology primarily solves the problem of disconnected, inefficient, and siloed business processes within organizations. By acting as a "central nervous system," SAP integrates data across finance.         

And this is just a small sample of the reviews available on G2 – there are many more that speak about the benefits of SAP BTP not only for integration, but application development, automation and support for clean core development too.

Springing forward with new badges

Having secured our Spring 2026 badges from G2, what next for SAP BTP? Obviously, Summer 2026 badges are now in view, and with support from our fabulous customers we shall hope to achieve these next. But first for the SAP BTP team, along with others from SAP, will be a focus on our very own Sapphire 2026 events, due to take place in Orlando and Madrid next month. The SAP BTP team will be presenting at both of these prestigious events, so if you happen to be joining one or the other, please make sure to come and say hello to the team on the show floor. And if you’re unable to join either of these events in-person, then that’s no problem, instead please join us at the SAP Sapphire Virtual event.

 With that I’ll sign off for this time, but meanwhile don’t forget to go to the G2 website and read more reviews for the customer-viewpoint on SAP BTP!

Chris Grundy, Product Marketing, SAP BTP

entry.title: Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP certified - Integration developer
entry.content:
You’ve probably worked with integrations that didn’t go as planned.  We’ve all been there.  APIs that don’t connect, flows that break, data that refuses to cooperate.

Now imagine walking into your next project with clarity.  Knowing exactly how to approach integration flows, APIs, and connectivity across SAP BTP.

That’s the goal of the Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified – Integration Developer.

In six live hours, we break down what you need for the C_CPI exam and, more importantly, what you need in real projects.  Plus, if you’ve already explored SAP Integration Suite, this is where everything starts to click.

The best part.  You don’t need to be an expert to start.  Just a solid foundation and the willingness to level up.

If certification is on your radar, this is a smart next move.

Register now and build confidence where it counts.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00009464.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #APIs #CloudIntegration #TechSkills

entry.title: Integrate CAP application as outbound channel for SAP Document AI
entry.content:
The Challenge

When integrating SAP Document AI as a document extraction engine into a broader BTP solution, one of the first hurdles you’ll encounter is the outbound callback mechanism. Document AI supports notifying an external system once extraction is complete — a great feature for building event-driven, asynchronous document processing pipelines.

However, there’s a subtle but important quirk: Document AI unconditionally performs a CSRF token preflight before sending the actual extraction result callback. It fires a GET request with the header X-CSRF-Token: Fetch to your callback endpoint regardless of whether CSRF protection is actually in place on the receiving end.

This behavior cannot be disabled in the Document AI configuration.

The solution? Register Express middleware directly during CAP’s bootstrap phase — before the framework takes over — and handle both the preflight and the callback there.

Architecture Overview

SAP Document AI
      │
      │  ① GET /callback/notify
      │     X-CSRF-Token: Fetch
      │◄─── X-CSRF-Token: <random-token>
      │
      │  ② POST /callback/notify
      │     X-CSRF-Token: <random-token>
      │     Body: notification payload
      ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│          CAP Application             │
│                                      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │     csrf-middleware.js         │  │
│  │  • Issues token on GET/HEAD    │  │
│  │  • Validates token on POST     │  │
│  └───────────────┬────────────────┘  │
│                  │                   │
│  ┌───────────────▼────────────────┐  │
│  │     callback-service.js        │  │
│  │  • Parses extraction payload   │  │
│  │  • Matches & finalises job     │  │
│  └────────────────────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────────────┘

The middleware intercepts both requests before CAP’s own routing and CSRF logic runs, acting as a transparent proxy layer between Document AI and your business logic.

Implementation Walkthrough

1. Hooking into the CAP Bootstrap

The key to this entire approach is cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => { ... }). This event fires after CAP has initialized the underlying Express application but before it registers its own middleware stack. This gives us direct, unmediated access to the raw Express app object.

The server.js is deliberately thin. Its only responsibility is wiring the CSRF middleware into the Express stack at the right moment:

'use strict';

const cds = require('@sap/cds');
const csrfMiddleware = require('./csrf-middleware');

cds.on('bootstrap', (app) => {
    app.use(csrfMiddleware);
});

module.exports = cds.server;

⚠️Exporting cds.server is essential — this tells CAP to use the standard server startup while still respecting your bootstrap customizations.

The actual callback business logic lives separately in callback-service.js, keeping concerns cleanly separated. The middleware handles the HTTP-level CSRF handshake; the service handles what to do with the payload.

2. The CSRF Middleware

The full middleware lives in csrf-middleware.js and handles two responsibilities: issuing tokens on preflight requests and validating tokens on incoming POST requests.

Token Lifecycle

Rather than returning a static or meaningless string, the middleware generates a cryptographically random token per preflight, stores it in memory with a TTL, and enforces single-use consumption:

// In-memory token store: token → expiry timestamp
const _tokens = new Map();
const TOKEN_TTL_MS = 10 * 60 * 1000; // 10 minutes

function generateToken() {
    const token = crypto.randomBytes(24).toString('hex');
    _tokens.set(token, Date.now() + TOKEN_TTL_MS);
    return token;
}

function validateToken(token) {
    if (!token) return false;
    const expiry = _tokens.get(token);
    if (!expiry) return false;
    if (Date.now() > expiry) { _tokens.delete(token); return false; }
    _tokens.delete(token); // single-use: consumed on first valid POST
    return true;
}

A periodic cleanup interval ensures expired tokens don’t accumulate indefinitely:

setInterval(() => {
    const now = Date.now();
    for (const [token, expiry] of _tokens.entries()) {
        if (expiry < now) _tokens.delete(token);
    }
}, 60000);

The Middleware Function

The middleware is path-scoped to /callback/* routes and handles each HTTP method appropriately:

function csrfMiddleware(req, res, next) {
    // Only apply to /callback paths
    if (!req.path.startsWith('/callback')) return next();

    const csrfHeader = req.headers['x-csrf-token'];

    // GET or HEAD preflight: issue a fresh token
    if ((req.method === 'GET' || req.method === 'HEAD') 
            && csrfHeader?.toLowerCase() === 'fetch') {
        const token = generateToken();
        log.debug(`CSRF preflight (${req.method}): issued token for ${req.path}`);
        res.set('X-CSRF-Token', token);
        res.set('Allow', 'GET,HEAD,POST');
        res.set('Cache-Control', 'no-cache, no-store');
        return res.status(200).end();
    }

    // POST: validate the token if present
    if (req.method === 'POST') {
        if (csrfHeader && !validateToken(csrfHeader)) {
            log.warn(`CSRF validation failed for POST ${req.path}`);
            return res.status(403).json({ 
                error: { message: 'CSRF token validation failed' } 
            });
        }
        log.debug(`CSRF token accepted for POST ${req.path}`);
    }

    next();
}

3. Handling the Callback Payload

Once the CSRF handshake is complete, Document AI posts the extraction results to /callback/notify. The actual processing logic is registered as a standard CAP service endpoint in callback-service.js.

What Document AI Sends

This is where it gets interesting. SAP Document AI schemas have a toggle that controls how much data is included directly in the callback payload. Depending on your schema configuration, you’ll encounter two patterns:

Option A — Full extraction result in the callback body

When the "include extraction result" toggle is enabled on your Document AI schema, the callback payload contains the complete extraction result directly in the DocumentResult field — serialized as a Python dictionary string representation (not valid JSON). This means you get all extracted field values, confidence scores, and metadata in a single push, with no need to make a follow-up API call to retrieve results.

DocumentResult = "{file_ID=abc-123, extracted_fields=[...], schema=InvoiceSchema, ...}"

⚠️ Note that this is Python repr() format, not JSON. Key-value pairs use = instead of :, and null values appear as None. Your callback handler needs to account for this — either with a dedicated parser or targeted regex extraction for the fields you need.

Option B — Lightweight notification only

When the toggle is disabled (or a simpler integration is preferred), the callback only contains a minimal notification with the NotificationObjectId. You can then use this ID to fetch the structured extraction result on demand via the Document AI DocumentService:

GET /document-service/v1/DocumentService/Documents({NotificationObjectId})/DocumentService.simpleStructure()

The DocumentService.simpleStructure() endpoint returns a clean, normalized JSON representation of the extracted fields — often the more practical choice if you don’t need the full raw extraction metadata and want a consistent, version-stable data contract between Document AI and your CAP application.

Setting Up the Callback Destination

With the CSRF middleware in place, the next step is making the callback URL reachable from SAP Document AI. This involves two things: defining the destination in your mta.yaml so it is automatically provisioned on deployment (ensuring automatic client credential updates), and then registering it as an outbound channel in the Document AI schema configuration UI.

Defining the Destination in mta.yaml

SAP BTP Destination Service allows you to declare destinations declaratively as part of your MTA deployment descriptor. This means the callback destination is created or updated automatically every time you deploy — no manual destination configuration in the BTP cockpit required.

Add a dedicated content deployer module to your mta.yaml:

# ─── Destination content deployer ─────────────────────────────────────────────
- name: docai-cap-dest-content
  type: com.sap.application.content
  requires:
    - name: docai-cap-dest # the name of your destination resource
      parameters:
        content-target: true
    - name: docai-cap-xsuaa # the name of your xsuaa resource
      parameters:
        service-key:
          name: docai-cap-xsuaa-key
    - name: srv-api
  parameters:
    content:
      subaccount:
        existing_destinations_policy: update
        destinations:
          - Name: CAP_DocAI_Callback
            Description: Callback destination for CAP app
            URL: ~{srv-url}/callback/notify
            Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
            TokenServiceInstanceName: docai-cap-xsuaa
            TokenServiceKeyName: docai-cap-xsuaa-key
            HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
  build-parameters:
    no-source: tru

A few things worth calling out:

~{srv-api/srv-url}/callback/notify
The URL is not hardcoded — it is resolved dynamically at deployment time using the srv-api provided parameters of your CAP server module. This means the destination always points to the correct URL regardless of which BTP space or region you deploy to, making the descriptor fully portable across landscapes.

OAuth2ClientCredentials
The callback destination is secured with OAuth2 Client Credentials, backed by the XSUAA instance of your CAP application. When Document AI calls the callback URL, it first fetches a Bearer token from XSUAA using the client credentials stored in docai-cap-scanner-xsuaa-key, and includes it in the Authorization header of every request — both the CSRF preflight GET and the actual callback POST. Your CAP application’s standard XSUAA-based authentication middleware validates this token automatically.

existing_destinations_policy: update
This ensures that redeployments update the destination in place rather than failing on a name conflict. Safe to use in CI/CD pipelines.

HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
Marks the destination as dynamically resolvable, which is required for runtime URL resolution via the destination service.

Configuring the Outbound Channel in SAP Document AI

Once deployed, the destination is available in your BTP subaccount and can be referenced directly from the SAP Document AI configuration UI. Navigate to Channels in Document AI and create an Outbound Channel ensuring the toggle for "Use SAP BTP Destination" is enabled.

 

Once saved, go to your schema, navigate to the Outbound Channel Configuration and add the just configured channel and toggle on/off the inclusion of your extraction results. Specifying the Document Status in which the channel shall be triggered allows you to either immediately forward the notification once the extraction is done (statusReview Needed) or only after a human validation (e.g. status Confirmed) .

With both the destination provisioned automatically via MTA and the outbound channel registered in the Document AI schema UI, the entire integration is reproducible from a single mbt build && cf deploy — no manual post-deployment steps required.

Conclusion

SAP Document AI is a powerful extraction service, but its fixed CSRF preflight behavior creates a real integration challenge when connecting to CAP applications or Integration Flows. By hooking into CAP’s bootstrap lifecycle and registering a focused, well-structured Express middleware, we can elegantly satisfy Document AI’s handshake requirements without patching CAP internals or weakening the application’s overall security posture.

The result is a clean two-layer architecture: the middleware owns the CSRF token lifecycle at the HTTP layer, and the callback service owns the business logic of matching jobs and processing extraction results — each with a single, clear responsibility.

This pattern is also broadly reusable: any time you need to expose a raw HTTP endpoint in a CAP application that bypasses the standard framework middleware (for webhooks, health checks, or third-party callbacks with unusual requirements), cds.on('bootstrap') is your entry point.

Happy building! 🚀

entry.title: Demystifying Model Context Protocol (MCP), Part 2: From Concept to Code
entry.content:
Recap and Next Steps

In Part 1, we laid the foundation for Model Context Protocol. We learnt MCP as an open standard that lets AI applications (hosts) talk to external systems through MCP clients and servers, instead of every team inventing a custom “tool API” for each integration.  We also focused on WHAT, WHY and WHEN of MCP. I strongly recommend going through Part 1 to understand the concepts of MCP and how it helps to streamline the integrations in agentic solutions.

In Part 2, we will focus more on:

  1. Building an MCP server
  2. Exposing tools
  3. Integrating with backend enterprise systems (e-commerce application)
  4. Debugging and Testing locally
  5. Deploying on cloud (Business Technology Platform)

To make this concrete, we’ll turn the ideas from Part 1 into something you can actually run end to end: A commerce MCP server that wraps Commerce OData APIs as agent‑ready tools.
By the end of Part 2, you will not just understand what a remote MCP server is, you will have one running on your local machine and can be deployed on cloud.

Commerce MCP Server (Architecture and Building Blocks)

The commerce-mcp-server project is a Python‑based MCP server built on FastMCP that exposes a set of tools wrapping Commerce (or any CAP‑based commerce) OData APIs. Instead of calling your Commerce APIs directly from the your agentic solution, the MCP server sits in the middle and offers agent‑ready tools like get_products, get_retailers etc. that are easy for MCP‑aware clients to discover and use. This is the concrete “MCP server” instance that brings the abstract concepts from Part 1 to life.

Building blocks of Commerce MCP Server

  1. Agentic Solutions + MCP Client: This is basically your Enterprise Agentic AI solution which embeds MCP client that talks to the commerce MCP server on behalf of the LLM.  MCP client discovers available tools exposed by the commerce MCP server and routes the LLM's request for data to the MCP server.
  2. Commerce MCP Server: MCP server is the application or a program which exposes the e-commerce capabilities to the AI Agent application through MCP standards.
  3. Tools: Tools are actions that the MCP server can perform. For example: "Get Product List", "Create Cart", "Add Product to Cart" etc. Tools gives host applications a structured set of capabilities.
  4. Transport Layer: MCP uses standard communication like STDIO for local and HTTP with Server‑Sent Events for remote servers.

The diagram below gives a overview of how commerce-mcp-server acts as a facade for the agentic AI solutions.

Jumping into the Code: How to Run It Yourself

Now that we’ve seen the architecture and building blocks, let’s get this MCP server running on your own machine and subaccount. The GitHub project already contains a detailed, step‑by‑step guide. in this section I will highlight the key choices and pitfalls, then point you to the exact README sections to follow.

Step 1: Pre-requisites

Before touching any code, verify the basics as called out in the sections

  1. https://github.com/avinashvaidya09/commerce-mcp-server/blob/main/README.md#pre-requisites
  2. https://github.com/avinashvaidya09/commerce-mcp-server/blob/main/README.md#local-set-up 

If you just want to play with the MCP server without touching BTP, the README’s “Option 1: Mock Backend (no BTP required)” path lets you skip the subaccount and destination setup for now.

Step 2: Decide - mock backend or live backend

The next decision point is: Do you want to hit real Commerce data, or just see the MCP server in action?

  1. For a quick start: Use mock backend as mentioned in the README file.

  2. For an end‑to‑end scenario against BTP:

    1. Use live backend.

    2. Then follow the VCAP SERVICES section to load service credentials into VCAP_SERVICES locally.

If this is your first time, start with Option 1 to understand MCP’s flow. Then come back and switch to Option 2 once your Commerce OData APIs are ready.

Step 3: Start MCP Server

Just start the MCP server to get started - https://github.com/avinashvaidya09/commerce-mcp-server/blob/main/README.md#mcp-server-on-local 

You can see the MCP inspector where you can see the tools and also test them.

 

The good news is: You don’t have to memorize any of these steps. The README file in the repository is intentionally written as a step‑by‑step playbook: from prerequisites and local setup, through mock vs live backend, all the way to BTP deployment and curl‑based debugging. Once you understand the high‑level choices we just walked through, you can simply follow the README top‑to‑bottom and you will end up with a working commerce MCP server and MCP Inspector showing all tools on your local.

Wrapping Up Part 2

At this point, you have seen how the commerce MCP server maps the MCP concepts from Part 1 into a concrete implementation. The architecture diagram and building blocks show you where the MCP server sits between MCP clients and your Commerce OData APIs. Rather than duplicating every command here, the README gives you a detailed, step‑by‑step path to go from zero to a running MCP server locally with mock data or against live Commerce services and finally deployed on BTP.

My recommendation: Read this blog once to get the mental model, then switch to the README and follow it top‑to‑bottom. If you do that, you will finish Part 2 not just with a theoretical understanding, but with a working commerce MCP server, tools like get_products, get_categories etc. exposed via MCP and at least one client (MCP Inspector, helper script, or curl) successfully talking to it.

In Part-3 of the series, we will move from standalone MCP server to integrating it with MCP client. We will create an agent and integrate MCP server, all using Joule Studio.

Disclaimer: This is not an official reference application or documentation. The thoughts outlined in this blog are based on my real world experience and learnings.

Feel free to “like“, “Share“, “Add a Comment” and to get more updates about my next blogs follow me!

References

  1. https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol
  2. https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/learn/architecture 
  3. https://gofastmcp.com/getting-started/quickstart

entry.title: SAP Green Ledger: Q1-26 Release Updates and Highlights
entry.content:
With this blog post, I would like to introduce a regular quarterly update series highlighting the latest innovations and enhancements in SAP Green Ledger. These updates provide an overview of new features and improvements delivered during the last quarter, helping you stay up to date with ongoing developments.

Short introduction: What is SAP Green Ledger? SAP Green Ledger supports organizations in integrating carbon into financial processes. It runs as an application on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and integrates with SAP S/4HANA Cloud–based finance systems. It enables organizations to monitor and document carbon emission impacts at transaction level and link emissions to financial objects. By using established financial structures such as accounts, company codes, and controlling objects, carbon emissions can be consistently assigned to business activities and financial categories. This supports transparent and auditable reporting, as well as data-driven operational and strategic decision-making.

With Q1 2026, SAP Green Ledger continues to evolve, supporting organizations in moving beyond emissions tracking toward managing carbon as a financial dimension. The features released in the first quarter of this year bring a range of enhancements in carbon liability management, regulatory readiness, and financial integration, along with improvements that make everyday processes more transparent and flexible. This blog post covers the following new features:

  • CBAM Functionality: Configuring Carbon Certificate Data and Managing Carbon Liabilities
  • Determining Mandatory Accounts for Carbon Certificates
  • Linking Carbon Data to Projects
  • Supporting Multiple SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Systems
  • Posting on Blocked Accounts
  • Reversing Manual Journal Entries
  • Further Updates and Improvements

 

CBAM Functionality: Configuring Carbon Certificate Data and Managing Carbon Liabilities

One of the key highlights this quarter is the support for carbon certificate data and carbon liabilities. Carbon certificates and liabilities play a central role in regulatory frameworks such as the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), where companies must cover embedded emissions with certificates that carry a financial value. Since the EU CBAM came into effect in January 2026, companies that import specific goods into the EU are now required to report embedded emissions in these imported goods and build up corresponding carbon liabilities. While the actual purchase of EU CBAM certificates will only begin in 2027, organizations already need to track and value these obligations today.

To support these new requirements, the new Configure Carbon Certificate Data app provides a centralized entry point for maintaining the data required for carbon certificate handling and valuation. You can create and maintain Emissions Trading System (ETS) prices, which represent the cost of emitting one metric ton of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2e). Since these prices fluctuate over time and directly impact liability valuation, maintaining them each calendar week helps ensure accuracy and alignment with market developments.

Maintaining Weekly EU ETS Prices (Illustrative Values Only)

In addition, you can display available company codes and access yearly CBAM factors. These factors determine the portion of embedded emissions that must be covered by CBAM certificates. By bringing ETS pricing, company context, and CBAM factors together, the app creates a consistent foundation that connects configuration with carbon data collection, liability calculation, and journal entry processing.

The flow is further supported by enhancements in the Manage Carbon Collections app. When creating a carbon collection, you can now indicate whether carbon certificates are required and maintain relevant information such as the number of certificates to surrender. This ensures that certificate requirements are captured consistently at the point of data entry. The information can then be used in later processing steps.

Carbon Certificate -relevant Carbon Collection

Building on this, the new Manage Carbon Liabilities app provides a dedicated space to view and evaluate carbon liabilities resulting from carbon collections that require certificates. You can analyze both the number of certificates to surrender as well as their corresponding monetary value based on ETS prices, reflecting the financial obligations associated with embedded emissions.

A key part of this process is fair value calculation. The system now calculates the fair value of carbon liabilities automatically for the current calendar year. This calculation is triggered whenever ETS prices are created, updated, or deleted, as well as when carbon collections requiring certificates are posted to the journal. Fair values are calculated by multiplying the number of certificates to surrender by the latest quarterly average ETS price. The calculated fair value covers the period from January 1 to the selected posting date within the year. At the same time, you can still trigger fair value calculations manually. This allows you to recalculate values when needed while keeping posting logic consistent.

Transparency is further improved through integration with journal entries as in the Manage Journal Entries app, you can review data originating from carbon collections. This includes, next to the line items for the collected GHG emissions, CBAM embedded emissions and certificate quantities now.

Journal Entries for CBAM-relevant Carbon Collection

Together, these capabilities provide greater transparency and control over how carbon liabilities are valued and reflected in financial processes. For more information, check SAP Help: Carbon Certificate Management. Looking ahead, we will continue to further develop support in this area. While the current focus is on supporting the calculation and management of carbon liabilities, future developments will address the next phase of EU CBAM. Starting from 2027, companies will be required to purchase CBAM certificates to cover their reported emissions. To support this, SAP Green Ledger will be extended with dedicated certificate management capabilities.

 

Determining Mandatory Accounts for Carbon Certificates

To support CBAM-relevant journal entry postings, account determination for carbon certificates has been enhanced with the Determine Mandatory Accounts app (formerly Manage Carbon-Only Accounts). It enables you to assign specific CBAM accounts to charts of accounts and company codes, supporting transactions such as liability recognition and fair value adjustments. This setup is mandatory for organizations subject to EU CBAM requirements. The app provides predefined CBAM account types for assets, liabilities, and operating expenses, which are typically maintained in a connected finance system and used in SAP Green Ledger. Postings to these accounts are generated automatically, ensuring alignment with underlying carbon processes.

Accounts Assignment for EU CBAM Accounts

Check out SAP Help for more information: Assign Carbon Certificate Accounts.

 

Linking Carbon Data to Projects

Carbon data can now be linked to projects and WBS (work breakdown structure) elements. A new WBS element field is available across the apps Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, Display Account Balances, and the Carbon Data Dashboard. This allows you to link carbon emissions directly to specific WBS elements. For that, project and WBS master data can now be imported from a connected finance system using our master data import capabilities and viewed in the new Display Projects app. This enhancement enables you to gain a more structured and detailed view of carbon data and makes it possible to analyze emissions at the level of individual projects and WBS elements.

Example Project in the Display Projects App

 

Supporting Multiple SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Systems

Another improvement is the increased flexibility in system integration with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management systems. Before, you could only connect one business system with the role SAP Sustainability Footprint Management to a SAP Green Ledger system. Now it is possible to create multiple business systems with that role. This allows you to work with more than one SAP Sustainability Footprint Management system, for example when importing carbon data from different sources. At the same time, each finance business system can be associated with only one SAP Sustainability Footprint Management system connected to a SAP Green Ledger system. This clear assignment ensures consistent and well-structured data integration. The setup is handled through business system configuration, where you define the system, assign its role, and maintain integration details such as the connected finance system.

 

Posting on Blocked Accounts

Posting behaviour has been made more flexible in SAP Green Ledger. Carbon collections can now be posted on accounts even if they are currently marked as blocked in the system. This is particularly relevant in scenarios where the account status may differ between SAP Green Ledger and the connected finance system. This can occur because account data in SAP Green Ledger is not updated in real time but is instead based on data imports from the finance system. As a result, the status in SAP Green Ledger may not always reflect the most current state.

Warning Message when Posting on a Blocked Account

Against this background, postings can still be carried out even if an account is indicated as blocked in SAP Green Ledger at a given point in time. Before posting, a warning message informs you if the account is marked as blocked. To ensure accuracy, you can verify the actual status of the account directly in the connected finance business system. Overall, this adjustment allows you to continue processes without interruption while still maintaining transparency and control over account usage.

 

Reversing Manual Journal Entries

You can now reverse manual journal entries in our Manage Journal Entries app, which makes it easier to correct postings and maintain accurate carbon data records. Reversing journal entries is possible if certain conditions are met, such as having the appropriate source type and not having been reversed previously. Once reversed, the system automatically creates corresponding journal entries in which debits and credits are swapped, ensuring that the financial impact is fully offset while preserving traceability.

The reversal information, including status, who initiated the reversal and when as well as the reversed journal entries, is available in the journal entry document, allowing you to track changes and maintain compliance with accounting standards. This enhancement helps ensure data accuracy while supporting controlled correction processes within SAP Green Ledger. For more details, have a look at SAP Help: Reverse Journal Entries.

 

Further Updates and Improvements

In addition to the key capabilities described above, several additional enhancements have been introduced to improve usability and transparency:

  • A new Journal Entry Source Type field is now available in key apps such as Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, and Display Account Balances and provides visibility into how journal entries were created (e.g., through allocation processes, liability valuation, carbon collection postings, imports, or manual entries and reversals).
  • Updates to the allocation functionality include the renaming of the allocation type Manual Allocation to Periodic Allocation and related UI adjustments.
  • Certain allocation options have been streamlined, such as the removal of the allocation type Account Balance Allocation from the Display Account Balances app.
  • External commenting is now available in the User Guide, enabling direct feedback and collaboration, see also Blog Post.

 

Additional Information

If you want to dive deeper into SAP Green Ledger, the SAP Help Portal is a good place to start. It provides detailed information on concepts, configuration, and application usage. You can also check the What’s New section, which is updated with every release and gives a complete overview of the latest additions. To see what's coming next, the SAP Road Map Explorer offers insight into planned features and upcoming innovations. It is updated regularly and outlines how the solution will continue to evolve.

entry.title: Beyond Joule Capabilities: A Knowledge Graph Driven Runtime for SAP Business AI
entry.content:
Beyond Joule Capabilities: A Knowledge Graph Driven Runtime for SAP Business AI

A working prototype and the idea behind it

IN THIS POST

  1. The scaling question
  2. The core idea
  3. The example
  4. Why this is different from Joule capabilities
  5. The architecture - KG as runtime brain
  6. Users do not think in system boundaries
  7. BDC - a potential path to faster coverage
  8. The honest complexity
  9. The prototype
  10. Closing thoughts

The scaling question

Today, building AI on SAP often means building capabilities one use case at a time. A new question introduces a new YAML definition, API binding, and deployment cycle. This model works well for many scenarios. But it raises a natural question: how does it scale as the variety and cross-system reach of user questions increases?

SAP's platform invitation is explicit: Joule as "an AI agent orchestrator," an agent builder for custom deployments, Business Data Cloud to "supercharge AI agents with the data that matters most, from any source." The infrastructure is there. This post explores one possible model for what to build on top of it - a working prototype running on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry, using a real A2A endpoint and a live PostgreSQL knowledge graph to answer retrieval questions without per-use-case authoring.

This is a personal exploration, not a product announcement. All views are my own. One constraint upfront: this covers read-oriented scenarios only - retrieval, explanation, analytics. Transactional write operations are out of scope.

The core idea

Joule owns the conversation. A2A owns the routing. The knowledge graph owns proven access patterns. OData owns the live data.

Each layer has one job. When a new system is added or a business term changes, only the relevant layer changes. The rest stays untouched.

This separation of concerns is the architectural backbone  and the reason the model handles question variability differently than a capability-per-use-case approach. The knowledge graph accumulates validated OData patterns over time; the A2A runtime draws on them at request time. Questions the KG already knows how to answer require no new authoring.

The example

A user says to Joule: "Show me my active purchase orders from supplier Bosch." No capability was pre-authored for that question.

Here is what happens:

 

Fig 1. The KG holds the pattern. The A2A layer matches the intent and composes the filter at runtime. Joule sees a card identical to any native capability.

The KG held the base pattern for active purchase orders. The slot extractor identified "Bosch" from the utterance and composed the OData filter at runtime.

The actual call:

The response template stored alongside the pattern in the KG rendered those records as a Joule card. The seam between Joule and the agent is invisible to the end user.

This leads to an important distinction from how Joule capabilities are typically built today.

Why this is different from Joule capabilities

SAP's Architecture Center documents two paths for extending Joule with custom agents: low-code via Joule Studio, and pro-code via the Bring Your Own Agent pattern -  where you expose an A2A-compliant HTTP endpoint and register it as a Joule Scenario. This prototype follows the pro-code path. What the knowledge graph adds is a runtime resolution layer on top of it: instead of one endpoint per use case, one endpoint resolves across all patterns the KG knows.

            TRADITIONAL JOULE CAPABILITY

             A2A + KNOWLEDGE GRAPH MODEL

  • One YAML file per use case, authored upfront
  • Hardcoded API, filter, and field selection
  • New question type = new capability version
  • Scale = more YAML
 
  • Patterns live in the KG, not in YAML files
  • Slot extraction composes filters at runtime
  • New supplier, year, or document: same pattern handles all
  • Scale = more usage
 

When to Use Each Approach

  • Traditional Joule Capabilities: Simple, well-defined use cases; single system queries; predictable user patterns
  • KG-Driven Runtime: Cross-system queries; high variation scenarios; exploratory business questions

The shift is from design-time authoring to runtime resolution. Governance changes accordingly: instead of "who owns this YAML?" the question becomes "who validated this OData pattern?" - answered by a domain lead approving a query-and-result pair, not a developer editing files.

Reference: SAP Architecture Center -  Integrating AI Agents with Joule documents the Bring Your Own Agent pro-code path, including A2A v0.3.0 protocol requirements and Joule Scenario registration.

The architecture - KG as runtime brain

Full architecture - A2A + Knowledge Graph on SAP BTP

Fig 2. End-to-end architecture: Joule routes via A2A to the self-hosted agent on BTP CF; the Knowledge Graph resolves intent and composes OData filters at runtime. Dashed boxes are planned / not yet GA.

 

 


Fig 3. The A2A layer is thin routing. The KG is where intelligence accumulates. The factory and runtime are the same flywheel - capability builds deposit patterns; the A2A runtime draws on them.

SAP's pro-code reference architecture for BTP agents also names a Knowledge Graph Engine - part of HANA Cloud - as the layer for encoding semantic business relationships. The PostgreSQL KG here is a developer-accessible analog of that concept: the same idea of accumulated, semantically enriched access patterns, running on a BTP-bound managed service rather than HANA Cloud.

Users do not think in system boundaries

"What is our total spend with supplier Bosch this year?" That question touches S/4HANA purchase orders, Ariba contracts, and Concur expense claims simultaneously. Users ask it as one question. Today it typically requires three separate lookups.

A2A offers a path toward answering it in one response - routing to specialized agents per system at runtime and composing the result. Each agent knows its own domain; none needs to know about the others. The same pattern applies across the SAP portfolio: procurement in S/4HANA, sourcing in Ariba, workforce in SuccessFactors, expense in Concur, external workforce in Fieldglass. The A2A routing layer is what makes cross-system answers possible without cross-system coupling.

One practical challenge emerges quickly: how to build initial KG coverage.

BDC - a potential path to faster coverage

The model works without BDC. But adoption will be slow. Building coverage through validated OData executions alone is a gradual process, and until the KG has enough patterns, too many intents fall back to "not yet supported."

BDC Data Products are designed to expose semantically enriched, consumption-ready models covering business definitions, domain taxonomy, and cross-entity relationships across SAP applications. If those models can be used to seed provisional KG entries, and live OData executions then promote them to validated, the cold-start problem becomes more manageable. Whether that seeding pipeline is straightforward to build depends on how BDC exposes its metadata in a given tenant, something worth exploring rather than assuming.

Raw OData tells you the shape of the data. BDC aims to tell you what the data means. That distinction could matter significantly when matching natural language to a query.

BDC also points toward a second channel - analytical questions that are difficult or impractical to answer through transactional OData alone, such as aggregated spend analysis or trend queries across periods. Those typically require pre-computed or aggregated data rather than a live API call. This is a natural conceptual extension of the same A2A routing layer, though not yet built in this prototype.

One important caveat: BDC's semantic model could provide a useful starting point, but tenant-specific customizations, restricted entity sets, and version differences would still require live validation before any pattern is trusted at runtime. BDC reduces the cold-start problem, it does not eliminate the validation step.

The honest complexity

Three areas are harder in practice than the architecture suggests.

Intent matching

PostgreSQL full-text search on intent keywords is fast but brittle on synonyms: "open orders" and "not invoiced" can mean the same thing; "cancelled" means different things in different modules. Production-grade matching would benefit from hybrid search combining full-text with embedding-based vector similarity so that semantic proximity compensates for lexical gaps.

Slot extraction

Resolving "Bosch" from a free-text utterance to a SupplierID sounds straightforward. In practice, users type partial names, aliases, or misspellings. Production-grade slot resolution needs alias handling, fuzzy matching, and potentially a lookup against master data, making it functionally a mini-MDM problem.

Authorization

The prototype authenticates via XSUAA JWT but calls OData using a service token, meaning it does not enforce row-level authorization per user. The right production path is principal propagation through BTP Destination Service - running the OData call under the user's own identity. This is on the roadmap but not yet implemented. The SAP Reference Architecture notes that the production Agent Gateway path uses IAS App2App tokens with named user context; the delta between that and a service token is the same gap this prototype still needs to close.

                                What works well

                                       Real limitations

  • Deterministic: every answer traces to a validated OData path - no hallucinated field names
  • Fast: KG lookup + OData call + render is sub-second
  • Auditable: every response traces to a specific pattern, entity, and filter
  • Composable: each system's agent is independently owned and evolvable

 

  • Cold start: BDC seeding may help; without some form of pre-seeding, early coverage is thin
  • Intent matching: full-text search alone misfires on synonyms and ambiguous phrasing
  • Slot resolution: aliases and fuzzy matching needed for production-grade accuracy
  • Authorization: row-level security requires principal propagation - not yet implemented
  • Schema drift: S/4HANA upgrades can silently break existing patterns
 

 

Governance model (in the prototype): Patterns are proposed automatically when a query succeeds. A domain lead reviews and promotes to validated=true before the pattern is trusted at runtime.
